message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is a progressive and debilitating respiratory disease with limited therapeutic options. We carried out genome-wide association (GWAS), post-GWAS and rare variant analyses utilising the whole genome sequencing data (WGS) from the 100,000 Genomes Project (100kGP) of a cohort of IPF participants (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  =586) to identify novel associations and potential drug targets. Meta-analysis combining 100kGP and published GWASs of IPF (total 11,746 cases and 1,416,493 controls) identified a novel association signal at the 1q21.2 locus (rs16837903, OR[95%CI]=0.88[0.85, 0.92],
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  =9.54×10
                  <jats:sup>−9</jats:sup>
                  ) which was also successfully replicated with independent data and linked to the probable effector gene
                  <jats:italic>MCL1. MCL1</jats:italic>
                  showed increased expression levels in IPF patients versus controls in alveolar epithelial type I cells. Despite its known antiapoptotic role, inhibition of
                  <jats:italic>MCL1 in vitro</jats:italic>
                  did not selectively deplete senescent cells, hinting at the complexity involved in targeting
                  <jats:italic>MCL1</jats:italic>
                  . Rare variant burden analysis identified
                  <jats:italic>ANGPTL7</jats:italic>
                  , a secreted glycoprotein involved in the regulation of angiogenesis, as a novel IPF candidate gene (OR[95%CI]=28.79 [8.51-97.43],
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  =6.73×10
                  <jats:sup>−8</jats:sup>
                  ). Transcriptome-wide association analysis (TWAS) revealed that overexpression of cell cycle regulator
                  <jats:italic>SERTAD2</jats:italic>
                  and nuclear importer
                  <jats:italic>TPNO3</jats:italic>
                  were associated with increased IPF risk. We also investigated shared genetic mechanisms between IPF with severe COVID-19 and expanded the list of shared genetic loci with three novel colocalised signals at 1q21.2, 6p24.3 and 16p13.3 with probable effector genes
                  <jats:italic>MCL1</jats:italic>
                  ,
                  <jats:italic>DSP and RHBDF1</jats:italic>
                  , implicating regulation of apoptosis, cell adhesion and epidermal growth factor signalling, respectively. By leveraging the genetic correlation between IPF and severe COVID-19 (
                  <jats:italic>
                    r
                    <jats:sub>g</jats:sub>
                  </jats:italic>
                  [95% CI]) = 0.39 [0.25-0.53]) through multi-trait meta-analysis, we further identified and replicated an additional novel candidate IPF signal at 2p16.1 with probable effector gene
                  <jats:italic>BCL11A</jats:italic>
                  , a regulator of haematopoiesis and lymphocyte development. Based on prioritized genes across analyses, we propose mechanisms mediating IPF disease risk and shared mechanisms between IPF and severe COVID-19, thereby expanding the potential for developing common treatments.
                </jats:p>
title: Common and rare variant analyses reveal novel genetic factors underlying Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis and shared aetiology with COVID-19
